title: The part that freezes during conflict and hates itself afterward
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
You go quiet when it matters most

I know this part. I've lived inside it more times than I can count.

You're in an argument. Something important is on the line. Your partner is upset, or your boss is criticizing you, or a friend crossed a line. And you feel it happening. That familiar shutdown. Your throat closes. Your thoughts scatter like leaves in wind. Your body goes still, heavy, almost numb. And you say nothing. Or you whisper something barely audible. Or you nod when you want to scream.

Then later, hours or days afterward, the self-hatred floods in. You replay the moment over and over. Why didn't I speak up? Why did I just sit there? What's wrong with me? You call yourself weak, pathetic, broken. You make promises to yourself that you'll never freeze again. But you do. Again and again.

Here's the thing. That freezing part isn't your enemy. It's not a character flaw. It's not evidence that you're broken. It's a survival strategy that kept you alive once. And it's still trying to protect you, even now, even when the danger is long gone.

Let's talk about what's actually happening when you freeze. Because it's not what you think.

The body's oldest emergency response

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us something crucial about the freeze response. It's not a failure of courage. It's a biological strategy wired deep in your nervous system. When your brain detects a threat that you can't fight and you can't flee, it activates the dorsal vagal pathway. This is the oldest part of our nervous system, the one we share with reptiles. It drops your heart rate, slows your breathing, and numbs your body. It's like a circuit breaker that flips when the system is overloaded.

Think about it. If you're a small child facing a raging parent, fighting back might get you hit harder. Running away might make things worse. But freezing? Going still and quiet? That often works. The threat passes. You survive. Your nervous system learns a powerful lesson: safety comes through collapse.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, calls this the "unfinished response." The energy your body mobilized to fight or flee gets locked in your muscles, your tissues, your nervous system. It never discharged. So your body stays stuck in that moment, ready to freeze again at the slightest hint of conflict. Right?

I've felt this in my own life more times than I can list. Arguments with partners where I went completely silent. Meetings where I had something important to say but sat there like a statue. Conversations with my parents where I reverted to that hollow, obedient child. And then the aftermath. The endless loop of self-flagellation.

But here's what changed for me. I stopped trying to force myself to be different. I started getting curious about the part that freezes. I started asking it, what are you trying to protect me from?

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and the freeze response helped me understand that this isn't something I can bully myself out of. It's something I need to befriend.

The shame spiral that follows the freeze

Here's the cruel twist. The freeze itself is painful enough. But the aftermath? The self-hatred that follows? That's often worse. And it keeps the whole cycle spinning.

You freeze during a conflict. Then you judge yourself for freezing. That judgment activates your nervous system again. You feel more threatened. So your body goes into an even deeper freeze next time. It's a loop. And shame is the fuel.

I've spent years studying this pattern in myself and in the people I write for. Janina Fisher, who integrates Internal Family Systems with trauma treatment, talks about how the part that hates you for freezing is actually another protective part. It's trying to motivate you to be different by shaming you. It believes that if it can make you feel bad enough, you'll finally change. But shame doesn't change behavior. It just deepens the freeze.

Stay with me here. I want you to notice something. Right now, as you read this, is there a part of you that's bracing? A part that's saying, yeah but I should be able to handle conflict by now. I'm too old for this. I've done so much work. Why am I still like this?

That's the shaming part. And it's exhausted. It's been working overtime for years, maybe decades, trying to whip you into shape. But it's using the wrong tool. You can't shame a nervous system into feeling safe. You can only build safety through compassion and understanding.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Nedra Glover Tawwab's work is direct and practical. But here's the thing. If you try to set boundaries while your nervous system is still in freeze mode, it won't work. You have to address the freeze first.

How to start working with the frozen part

So what do you actually do? How do you work with a part that's been protecting you by going silent?

First, you stop trying to get rid of it. I know that sounds counterintuitive. You want this part gone. You want to be someone who speaks up, who stands their ground, who doesn't collapse into silence. I get it. I wanted that too. But fighting the freeze only makes it stronger.

Instead, start with curiosity. Next time you feel that shutdown coming, or next time you notice it after the fact, try this. Take a breath. Not a deep breath, just a normal one. And ask yourself, what does this part need me to know? Not what's wrong with me, but what's happening inside me right now?

You might notice sensations. Tightness in your chest. A hollow feeling in your stomach. Numbness in your hands. That's the freeze response in your body. And it's not your enemy. It's information.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's framework helped me see that the freezing part isn't the whole of me. It's one part. And parts can change. They can learn new roles.

Second, you practice small doses of presence. You don't start by confronting your partner about the big issue. You start by noticing that you're freezing in a conversation with a cashier. Or that you went silent during a minor disagreement with a friend. You acknowledge it. You don't judge it. You just say, oh, there's that part. Hi.

Third, you learn to track your window of tolerance. Dan Siegel coined this term. It's the zone where you can stay present and responsive without flipping into fight, flight, or freeze. When you're close to the edge of that window, your body starts sending signals. Maybe your shoulders tense. Maybe your breathing gets shallow. Maybe you feel a familiar hollow sensation. That's your chance. That's the moment before the freeze fully takes over. If you can catch it early, you can take a small action. A hand on your chest. A step back. A sip of water. Something tiny that says to your nervous system, I see you. I'm here.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Lindsay Gibson's work helps explain why so many of us developed the freeze response in the first place. When your emotions were dismissed or punished, freezing became the safest option. Your body learned that lesson well.

What healing actually looks like

I want to be honest with you. Healing the freeze response isn't about never freezing again. That's a fantasy sold by the wellness industry. Real healing looks different. It's more subtle and more .

Healing looks like freezing for 20 minutes instead of two hours. It looks like feeling the shutdown coming and being able to say, I need a moment. It looks like freezing during an argument and then, later, being able to come back and say what you needed to say. It looks like the freeze happening less often, and when it does happen, the shame spiral lasting minutes instead of days.

Wild, right? The goal isn't perfection. The goal is a better relationship with this part. A relationship where you don't hate yourself for having a survival response that kept you safe.

I still freeze sometimes. I'm not going to pretend otherwise. But I don't call myself weak anymore. I don't spend hours in self-hatred. I notice the freeze. I put a hand on my chest. I say to myself, it's okay. This part is trying to protect me. I'm safe now. And slowly, over time, my nervous system has started to believe me.

Your frozen part isn't your enemy. It's a scared part that learned to disappear to survive. And it can learn new things. It can learn that you're an adult now. That you have resources. That you can handle conflict without being destroyed. But it needs you to show it, not tell it. Through presence. Through patience. Through the kind of consistent, gentle attention that trauma never got.

Sit with that. Notice what just happened in your body. Maybe there's a softening. Maybe there's resistance. Both are fine. Both are parts. And they're all welcome here.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
